perturbation.original_text: (f) “Personal Data” has the same meaning assigned to this phrase as in Section 2(1) of the Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore .
perturbation.changed_text: (f) “Personal Data” shall mean any data related to an identifiable individual, regardless of whether the individual can be identified directly from that data alone.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract correctly refers to the definition in the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) of Singapore. By changing the definition to a more generic one, we create a conflict, as the PDPA has a specific scope and conditions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Personal Data' beyond what is legally defined by Section 2(1) of the PDPA, leading to potential misinterpretations and non-compliance with the actual requirements of the Act. The PDPA contains specific inclusions and exclusions that are not reflected in the new definition.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Personal Data' beyond the specific legal definition provided by Section 2(1) of the PDPA, as the Law Explanation states. This creates a 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from the legally recognized definition, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The original text accurately refers to the PDPA definition, while the change introduces a generic definition. The scraped snippets confirm the existence of the PDPC and its role in administering the PDPA, underlining the importance of adhering to its specific definitions rather than using a general one.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 The Company shall, in relation to Personal Data: - (a) ensure that it has, in relation to all Personal Data obtained and/or collected by it , fully complied with all req uirements of the Personal Data Protection Act (No. 26 of 2012);
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 The Company shall, in relation to Personal Data: - (a) ensure that it attempts to comply with the general spirit of data protection principles;
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates full compliance with the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA). The modified version weakens this requirement to simply 'attempting to comply with the general spirit,' creating a contradiction. Full compliance is legally required, not optional.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the PDPA imposes obligations on organisations to protect personal data. Changing the contractual obligation to 'attempting to comply' directly contradicts the mandatory nature of these obligations under the Act, potentially making the contract unenforceable in this aspect.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates full compliance with the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA). The changed text weakens this to 'attempting to comply with the general spirit,' directly contradicting the mandatory nature of the obligations imposed by Section 4 of the PDPA. The change introduces language that would likely fail legal scrutiny, potentially making the contract unenforceable. Although the scraped snippet does not contain Section 4, the PDPC website information directs users to resources regarding the PDPA, showing that the act is in effect and the changed text undermines the act.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 (d) not transfer or allow the Personal Data to be transferred, outside of Singapore, unless expressly instructed or authorized by IHiS; and
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 (d) may transfer Personal Data outside of Singapore without explicit instructions, provided the Company deems the recipient country to have adequate data protection laws; and
perturbation.explanation: The original text prohibits data transfer outside Singapore without explicit authorization from IHiS. The modified version allows transfers based on the Company's assessment of the recipient country's laws, directly contradicting the PDPA's requirements for cross-border data transfer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 26 of the PDPA regulates cross-border transfer of personal data, requiring organizations to ensure that the recipient provides a standard of protection comparable to the PDPA. Allowing the Company to unilaterally decide if a country's laws are 'adequate' circumvents the requirements and IHiS's control over data transfer as intended by the PDPA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the company to transfer personal data outside Singapore based on its own assessment of the recipient country's data protection laws, directly contradicting the original contract's requirement for explicit authorization from IHiS. The LLM explanation states that this circumvents the PDPA requirements for cross-border data transfer, as the company's unilateral decision on adequacy doesn't ensure a standard of protection comparable to the PDPA. While the provided scraped law snippets do not directly reference Section 26 of the PDPA, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation outline that the change introduces non-compliance, by allowing transfers without explicit authorization. This represents a misalignment with PDPA standards for data protection, meeting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 The Company shall within seven days of : (f) completion of the Purpose ; or (g) receipt of a written request from IHiS; or (h) expiry or termination of the Agreement, return to IHiS all documents and materials (and all copies thereof) containing the IHiS’ Confidential Information or destroy the same, and certify in writing to IHiS that it has complied with the requirements of this sub -clause .
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 The Company shall, at its discretion, upon (f) completion of the Purpose; or (g) receipt of a written request from IHiS; or (h) expiry or termination of the Agreement, either return to IHiS all documents and materials (and all copies thereof) containing the IHiS’ Confidential Information or destroy the same.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the return OR destruction of confidential information within seven days and certification of compliance. This alteration removes the obligation to certify compliance and makes the action discretionary, undermining IHiS's control over its confidential data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11 of the PDPA mandates reasonable security arrangements to protect personal data. Removing the obligation to return or destroy data in all cases, coupled with the removal of the certification requirement, weakens the security arrangements and potentially violates the requirement for reasonable protection under the PDPA. It weakens the data retention policy.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated the return OR destruction of confidential information and certification of compliance, while the modified contract makes the action discretionary and removes the certification requirement. The Law Explanation states that Section 11 of the PDPA mandates reasonable security arrangements to protect personal data and that removing the obligation to return or destroy data in all cases, coupled with the removal of the certification requirement, weakens the security arrangements and potentially violates the requirement for reasonable protection under the PDPA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a PDPC webpage, which provides links to "Overview of the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA)". The change directly contradicts the PDPA as described in the law explanation.
